questions.question: Define thrashing and explain the condition that leads to thrashing in a virtual memory system.
keyPoints: Thrashing is excessive paging activity causing severe performance degradation
It occurs when processes do not have enough frames to hold their working sets
Results in high page fault rate where CPU spends more time on page I/O than execution

questions.question: Explain the Working Set Model for controlling thrashing. What is the parameter τ (tau)?
keyPoints: Working set is the set of pages referenced in the last τ time units
τ (tau) is the time window that defines the working set
System must allocate enough frames to accommodate each process's working set
If total working set size exceeds available frames, thrashing occurs

questions.question: Describe the Page Fault Frequency (PFF) control strategy for thrashing prevention.
keyPoints: PFF directly monitors each process's page fault rate
Upper threshold: if exceeded, allocate more frames to process
Lower threshold: if below, reclaim excess frames from process
This maintains page fault rate within acceptable range

questions.question: Explain the relationship between degree of multiprogramming and CPU utilization in a virtual memory system.
keyPoints: Initially, as degree increases, CPU utilization increases (more processes keep CPU busy)
At optimal point, CPU utilization is maximum
Beyond this point, adding more processes causes thrashing
CPU utilization drops sharply because time spent on page faults exceeds execution time

questions.question: List three symptoms of thrashing and explain why each occurs.
keyPoints: Very high page fault rate: processes constantly need pages not in memory
Very low CPU utilization: CPU idle waiting for page I/O operations
High disk I/O activity: constant swapping of pages in and out
Processes in blocked/waiting state: waiting for page faults to be serviced

questions.question: Differentiate between local and global page replacement in the context of thrashing.
keyPoints: Local replacement: process can only use its own allocated frames
Global replacement: any process can take frames from any other process
Local replacement helps contain thrashing within one process
Global replacement can accelerate thrashing as processes steal frames from each other

questions.question: What strategies can be employed to prevent or reduce thrashing?
keyPoints: Adjust degree of multiprogramming (suspend some processes)
Use working set model or PFF control
Implement priority-based allocation
Use local replacement instead of global
Apply pre-paging to reduce fault rate
Ensure minimum frame allocation per process

questions.question: Explain the thrashing phenomenon with the help of a diagram showing the relationship between CPU utilization and degree of multiprogramming. How can the system recover from thrashing?
keyPoints: Draw and explain thrashing curve: utilization rises with degree, peaks at optimal point, then drops sharply
Optimal point is just before thrashing begins
Recovery: suspend/swamp out some processes to reduce memory demand
Or allocate more frames if possible
Or use PFF/working set to rebalance frame allocation
Goal is to return to optimal operating point

questions.question: Explain the working set model for thrashing control. How does the choice of τ (window size) affect thrashing? What happens if τ is too small or too large?
keyPoints: Working set W(t,τ) = pages referenced in time interval (t-τ, t)
Each process needs frames equal to its working set size
If sum of working sets > available memory, thrashing occurs
If τ too small: working set may be too small, doesn't reflect actual needs, leads to thrashing
If τ too large: working set includes unnecessary pages, wastes memory
Optimal τ captures actual memory needs of process

questions.question: Compare and contrast Working Set Model and Page Fault Frequency (PFF) control for thrashing prevention. Which approach would be more effective in a system with highly variable reference patterns?
keyPoints: Working Set Model: tracks pages in recent time window, proactive approach
PFF: monitors actual page fault rate, reactive approach
Working set more stable but depends on proper τ selection
PFF directly responds to actual fault behavior
For variable patterns: PFF may be better as it adapts to actual behavior
Working set might be too rigid or too loose depending on τ
